attrs.transmission: Bite of sandfly (Phlebotomus / Lutzomyia); zoonosis — DOG is main reservoir for L. infantum
attrs.pathogenesis: Amastigotes survive & multiply inside macrophages → spread to reticuloendothelial organs
attrs.disease: Visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar) — hepatosplenomegaly, fever, cachexia, anemia, ↑IgG; ~80% fatal untreated
attrs.virulence: Leishmanolysin; cysteine protease B; inhibits Th1 (favors Th2)
attrs.diagnosis: Histology (amastigotes in macrophages); NAAT; serology
attrs.treatment: Liposomal amphotericin B; pentavalent antimonials; miltefosine
attrs.special: Kinetoplast = circular mitochondrial DNA; promastigote in sandfly, amastigote in humans

attrs.transmission: Sandfly bite
attrs.pathogenesis: Localized macrophage infection in the skin; Th1-controlled
attrs.disease: Cutaneous leishmaniasis (oriental sore) — ulcer with raised edges, heals leaving a scar
attrs.diagnosis: Skin biopsy (amastigotes); NAAT
attrs.special: Cutaneous form; L. braziliensis → mucocutaneous (espundia)

attrs.transmission: Bite of the tsetse fly (Glossina)
attrs.pathogenesis: Extracellular in blood/lymph → crosses into CNS; antigenic variation evades immunity
attrs.disease: African trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness): Stage 1 fever/lymphadenopathy → Stage 2 CNS (somnolence, coma, death)
attrs.virulence: Variant surface glycoprotein switching → immune evasion
attrs.diagnosis: Blood/lymph/CSF smear; PCR; clinical & geographic context
attrs.special: Rhodesiense (East Africa) more acute/virulent than gambiense (West Africa)

attrs.transmission: Reduviid ("kissing") bug — defecates while feeding; rubbed into bite/mucosa; also transfusion/transplacental
attrs.pathogenesis: Intracellular amastigotes in cardiac/GI tissue
attrs.disease: Chagas disease — acute (often asymptomatic, Romaña sign) → chronic cardiomyopathy, megaesophagus/megacolon
attrs.diagnosis: Acute: blood trypomastigotes/PCR; chronic: serology
attrs.special: American trypanosomiasis; congenital & transfusion transmission possible

attrs.transmission: Fecal-oral (contaminated water/food); cysts are environmentally resistant
attrs.pathogenesis: Trophozoites attach to duodenal/jejunal villi → villous flattening, malabsorption (non-invasive)
attrs.disease: Giardiasis — foul-smelling fatty (steatorrhea) diarrhea, cramps, bloating; chronic carrier common
attrs.diagnosis: Stool ova & parasites (cysts/trophozoites); stool antigen ELISA; duodenal aspirate
attrs.special: Flagellate; cyst is the infective form

attrs.transmission: Sexual (humans are the only host/reservoir)
attrs.pathogenesis: Adheres to genitourinary epithelium; anaerobic; proteases + ROS cause local damage
attrs.disease: Trichomoniasis — yellow-green frothy malodorous discharge, "strawberry cervix" (women); often asymptomatic in men
attrs.diagnosis: Wet mount (motile trophozoites); NAAT/PCR; culture
attrs.special: Most common non-viral STI; exists ONLY as trophozoite (no cyst); increases HIV transmission

attrs.transmission: Bite of female Anopheles mosquito; also transfusion/transplacental
attrs.pathogenesis: Exo-erythrocytic phase in hepatocytes → erythrocytic cycle (merogony) → cyclic RBC lysis
attrs.disease: Malaria — cyclic fever/chills, hemolytic anemia, splenomegaly; P. falciparum = severe (cerebral malaria, ARDS)
attrs.diagnosis: Thick & thin blood smear (Giemsa); rapid antigen tests; PCR
attrs.treatment: Chloroquine (sensitive); ACT; primaquine kills hypnozoites of vivax/ovale
attrs.special: Infective stage = SPOROZOITE; "hypnozoite" = dormant liver form of P. vivax/ovale → relapses; falciparum most severe

attrs.transmission: Ixodes tick (same vector as Lyme disease); also transfusion
attrs.pathogenesis: Intraerythrocytic replication → hemolysis
attrs.disease: Babesiosis — often asymptomatic; fever, hemolytic anemia; severe/fatal in asplenic/immunocompromised
attrs.diagnosis: Blood smear (Giemsa) — "Maltese cross" tetrad; serology/PCR
attrs.special: Maltese-cross tetrad in RBCs

attrs.infectiveStage: Oocyst (cat feces / soil) or tissue cyst (raw meat); tachyzoite
attrs.transmission: Ingestion of oocysts (raw vegetables, cat litter) or tissue cysts (raw/undercooked meat); transplacental; transfusion/transplant
attrs.pathogenesis: Tachyzoite invades cells, forms parasitophorous vacuole, inhibits lysosomal fusion → tissue cysts (bradyzoites) = lifelong latency
attrs.disease: Usually asymptomatic / mononucleosis-like; congenital toxoplasmosis (chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, intracranial calcification); reactivation encephalitis in HIV
attrs.virulence: Intracellular survival; converts to bradyzoite cysts (immune-driven)
attrs.diagnosis: Serology IgM/IgG (avidity in pregnancy); PCR (ocular/amniotic/CSF); brain imaging in immunocompromised
attrs.treatment: Often none (immunocompetent); pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid
attrs.special: CAT = definitive host (sexual cycle); tachyzoite = active form, bradyzoite = cyst; pyrimethamine inhibits DHFR (folate synthesis)

attrs.transmission: Oro-fecal; contaminated water (resistant to chlorination); farm animals
attrs.pathogenesis: Attaches to small-intestinal epithelium; auto-infection
attrs.disease: Cryptosporidiosis — self-limiting watery diarrhea (immunocompetent); profuse chronic diarrhea in HIV/immunocompromised
attrs.virulence: Intestinal epithelial attachment; severity depends on immune status
attrs.diagnosis: Modified acid-fast (Ziehl-Neelsen) stain of stool; antigen ELISA; PCR
attrs.treatment: Nitazoxanide; supportive rehydration; restore immunity (ART in HIV)
attrs.special: Chlorine-resistant; major waterborne outbreak agent

attrs.transmission: Fecal-oral (contaminated water/food)
attrs.pathogenesis: Trophozoites invade colonic mucosa (flask-shaped ulcers); can disseminate via portal vein to liver
attrs.disease: Amebiasis — amebic (bloody) dysentery; extraintestinal liver abscess (RUQ pain)
attrs.diagnosis: Stool antigen ELISA (distinguishes from E. dispar); PCR/NAAT; serology; ultrasound/CT for abscess
attrs.special: Primary localization = COLON; produces cysts; only E. histolytica (not E. dispar) is invasive

attrs.transmission: INHALATION of warm fresh water through nasal mucosa (swimming/diving); NOT person-to-person
attrs.pathogenesis: Crosses cribriform plate directly to the brain (olfactory)
attrs.disease: Primary amoebic meningoencephalitis (PAM) — rapid, fulminant; >97% mortality
attrs.diagnosis: CSF wet mount — motile amoebae; PCR; biopsy (often post-mortem)
attrs.special: Primary localization = BRAIN; warm freshwater; "brain-eating amoeba"

attrs.transmission: Direct contact — contaminated contact-lens solution, soil, water (skin/corneal entry)
attrs.pathogenesis: Corneal or, via skin/sinus, hematogenous CNS spread
attrs.disease: Acanthamoeba keratitis (contact-lens wearers); granulomatous amoebic encephalitis (GAE) in immunocompromised
attrs.diagnosis: Corneal scraping/culture; confocal microscopy; PCR; brain biopsy (GAE)
attrs.treatment: Keratitis: topical PHMB + chlorhexidine ± voriconazole; GAE: multi-drug, poor prognosis
attrs.special: Classic in contact-lens wearers; both trophozoite & cyst forms

attrs.transmission: Fecal-oral (autoinfection); eggs become infective within hours; survive weeks in environment
attrs.pathogenesis: Females migrate to perianal skin at night to lay eggs
attrs.disease: Enterobiasis — perianal nocturnal itching (hallmark); often mild/asymptomatic
attrs.diagnosis: Scotch-tape test (perianal, morning); eggs flattened on one side
attrs.treatment: Albendazole / mebendazole (repeat in 2–3 weeks); treat household
attrs.special: Most common helminth in temperate regions; diagnosed by tape test

attrs.transmission: Fecal-oral (ingestion of embryonated eggs from soil)
attrs.pathogenesis: Larvae migrate liver→heart→lungs→trachea→swallowed; adults in small intestine
attrs.disease: Ascariasis — often mild; heavy load → intestinal obstruction; Loeffler pneumonia (pulmonary migration)
attrs.diagnosis: Stool — round fertilized eggs with thick shell
attrs.special: Infective form = fertilized egg

attrs.transmission: Filariform larvae penetrate skin (walking barefoot on contaminated soil)
attrs.pathogenesis: Skin → blood → lungs → swallowed → attach to small-intestinal mucosa and suck blood
attrs.disease: Hookworm disease (ancylostomiasis) — iron-deficiency anemia, malnutrition; pruritic dermatitis at entry; pulmonary phase
attrs.diagnosis: Stool — oval thin-shelled eggs; CBC (microcytic anemia, eosinophilia)
attrs.special: Symptoms = ANEMIA and malnutrition; larvae penetrate skin

attrs.transmission: Filariform larvae penetrate skin; UNIQUE autoinfection cycle (persists for decades)
attrs.pathogenesis: Skin→lung→intestine; rhabditiform larvae can transform inside host and reinfect
attrs.disease: Strongyloidiasis — often asymptomatic; HYPERINFECTION/disseminated (sepsis, meningitis, pneumonia) in immunocompromised (high mortality)
attrs.diagnosis: Larvae in stool (not eggs); stool PCR; serology; eosinophilia (absent in hyperinfection)
attrs.special: Autoinfection → hyperinfection syndrome; screen before immunosuppression

attrs.transmission: Ingestion of raw/undercooked meat containing encysted larvae
attrs.pathogenesis: Larvae mature in gut → females release larvae → enter blood → encyst in skeletal muscle (and can affect CNS/heart)
attrs.disease: Trichinosis — intestinal phase (diarrhea) → muscle phase (myalgia, periorbital edema, marked eosinophilia); myocarditis/CNS in severe
attrs.diagnosis: Clinical + dietary history; serology; muscle biopsy
attrs.treatment: Mebendazole/albendazole for adult worms; corticosteroids; NO effective drug for encysted larvae
attrs.special: From raw meat; can damage the CNS; encysted larvae untreatable

attrs.transmission: Mosquito bite (Culex/Anopheles/Aedes); larvae enter near the bite site
attrs.pathogenesis: Adults lodge in lymphatics → microfilariae in blood (nocturnal); lymphatic blockage
attrs.disease: Lymphatic filariasis → ELEPHANTIASIS (chronic lymphedema); acute lymphangitis; tropical pulmonary eosinophilia
attrs.diagnosis: Microfilariae in blood (nocturnal sample); serology (IgG4); PCR
attrs.special: Causes elephantiasis; infection via MOSQUITO bite (not hair follicles); harbors Wolbachia

attrs.transmission: Bite of Simulium blackfly (breeds in fast-flowing rivers)
attrs.pathogenesis: Adults in subcutaneous nodules; microfilariae migrate through skin & to the eye; dying microfilariae (releasing Wolbachia) cause inflammation
attrs.disease: Onchocerciasis (river blindness) — subcutaneous nodules, dermatitis, ocular damage → blindness
attrs.keyVF: Microfilarial migration to eye; Wolbachia-driven inflammation
attrs.diagnosis: Skin snip (microfilariae); nodule biopsy; slit-lamp eye exam
attrs.special: Blindness risk determined by the ANATOMICAL LOCATION of the blackfly bite (near the eyes)

attrs.transmission: Bite of Chrysops (deer/mango) fly — bites by day
attrs.pathogenesis: Adults migrate in subcutaneous tissue & across the eye; microfilariae in blood (diurnal)
attrs.disease: Loiasis — transient Calabar swellings; visible worm migrating across the conjunctiva
attrs.diagnosis: Microfilariae in daytime blood; visible adult worm; eosinophilia
attrs.treatment: Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) ± steroids; surgical removal of ocular worm
attrs.special: Worm visible crossing the eye

attrs.transmission: Drinking water containing infected copepods (cyclops)
attrs.pathogenesis: Larvae mature; adult female migrates to skin (usually lower limb) → painful blister that bursts to release larvae into water
attrs.disease: Dracunculiasis — painful cutaneous ulcer/blister on lower limbs; secondary infection/tetanus risk
attrs.diagnosis: Clinical (worm visible at ulcer)
attrs.treatment: No drug — mechanical extraction (slow winding around a stick over days)
attrs.special: Near global eradication; subcutaneous ulcer of the lower limb

attrs.transmission: Eating raw/undercooked pork with cysticerci → taeniasis; ingesting EGGS (food contaminated with human feces / autoinfection) → cysticercosis
attrs.pathogenesis: Adult tapeworm in small intestine (taeniasis); eggs → larvae encyst in tissues (cysticercosis)
attrs.disease: Taeniasis (often mild); cysticercosis — cysts in brain (neurocysticercosis → seizures), eyes, muscle
attrs.virulence: Larval cysts in CNS/eye → mass effect, seizures
attrs.diagnosis: Stool — eggs/proglottids, antigen (taeniasis); CT/MRI of calcified cysts + serology (cysticercosis)
attrs.special: Cysticercosis is acquired by ingesting eggs in human-feces-contaminated FOOD (not by eating pork)

attrs.transmission: Eating raw/undercooked beef containing cysticerci
attrs.pathogenesis: Adult tapeworm attaches in small intestine
attrs.disease: Taeniasis — usually mild GI symptoms; proglottids may migrate out of the anus
attrs.diagnosis: Stool — proglottids/eggs; ELISA
attrs.special: Does NOT cause cysticercosis in humans (unlike T. solium)

attrs.transmission: Ingestion of eggs from DOG (definitive host) feces; sheep = intermediate host; humans = accidental intermediate host
attrs.pathogenesis: Oncospheres penetrate intestine → blood → form slow-growing unilocular hydatid cysts (mainly liver, also lung)
attrs.disease: Cystic echinococcosis (hydatid disease) — expanding cysts; rupture → anaphylaxis & dissemination
attrs.diagnosis: Imaging (CT/MRI/US) + serology; avoid aspiration (or PAIR with care)
attrs.special: HYDATID CYSTS mainly in the LIVER; dog-sheep cycle

attrs.transmission: Freshwater snails (intermediate host) release cercariae → penetrate human skin directly (swimming/wading)
attrs.pathogenesis: Adults in venous plexuses; eggs lodge in tissue → granulomas/fibrosis
attrs.disease: Schistosomiasis — S. haematobium → hematuria, bladder fibrosis/cancer (eggs in URINE); S. mansoni/japonicum → hepatosplenic, portal hypertension (eggs in feces); cercarial dermatitis; Katayama fever
attrs.diagnosis: Eggs in urine (haematobium) or stool (mansoni/japonicum); antigen; serology
attrs.special: S. HAEMATOBIUM eggs are shed in URINE (→ bladder), not feces — bladder cancer risk

attrs.transmission: Ingestion of contaminated aquatic plants (e.g. watercress); snails are intermediate host; sheep reservoir
attrs.pathogenesis: Larvae penetrate intestinal wall → liver parenchyma → mature in bile ducts
attrs.disease: Fascioliasis — acute (larval liver migration: fever, RUQ pain, eosinophilia) → chronic biliary obstruction/cholangitis
attrs.diagnosis: Eggs in stool; ELISA (excretory/secretory antigen); imaging
attrs.special: Acquired by eating contaminated aquatic plants (watercress)

questions.stem: Kinetoplasts are:
questions.explanation: The kinetoplast is a specialized mitochondrion containing a network of circular DNA (kinetoplastid protozoa).

questions.stem: The Leishmania of the donovani family are responsible for:
questions.explanation: L. donovani/infantum cause visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar).

questions.stem: What is the main animal reservoir of Leishmania infantum (visceral leishmaniasis in the Mediterranean basin)?
questions.explanation: The dog is the main reservoir of L. infantum in the Mediterranean.

questions.stem: Trypanosoma brucei:

options.t: Is transmitted by the tsetse fly
The rhodesiense form is more virulent than the gambiense form
Is transmitted by the bite of a mosquito

questions.explanation: T. brucei is tsetse-fly-borne, evades immunity by antigenic (VSG) variation, and rhodesiense is the more acute/virulent form.

questions.stem: How is Trypanosoma brucei transmitted to humans?

options.t: Through the bite of a fly (tsetse fly)
Through the bite of a mosquito

questions.explanation: The tsetse fly (Glossina) transmits T. brucei.

questions.stem: Which of the following protozoa causes the most severe form of malaria?
questions.explanation: P. falciparum causes severe malaria (cerebral malaria, high parasitemia).

questions.stem: Which is the infective stage of Plasmodium in humans?
questions.explanation: The mosquito injects sporozoites, which infect hepatocytes.

questions.stem: To which protozoan does the term "hypnozoite" refer?
questions.explanation: Hypnozoites are dormant liver stages of P. vivax/ovale → relapses.

questions.stem: Where does the primary localization of Naegleria fowleri occur in the body?
questions.explanation: N. fowleri travels via the olfactory route to the brain → primary amoebic meningoencephalitis.

questions.stem: How is Naegleria fowleri transmitted to humans?

options.t: Through (nasal) contact with contaminated warm fresh water
Through the bite of an infected mosquito
Through the bodily fluids of an infected person

questions.explanation: Acquired by warm freshwater entering the nose (swimming/diving).

questions.stem: Eating raw vegetables can lead to the risk of infection with which parasite?
questions.explanation: Oocysts on raw vegetables (and cat litter) transmit Toxoplasma gondii.

questions.stem: In which host does sexual reproduction of Toxoplasma gondii take place?
questions.explanation: The cat is the definitive host; sexual reproduction occurs in the feline intestine.

questions.stem: Which statements regarding infection with Toxoplasma gondii are correct?

options.t: Fetal infection is more likely (but milder) early, and more severe later in gestation

questions.explanation: Toxoplasma infection is usually asymptomatic, drives a protective Th1 response; the cat (not humans) is the definitive host.

questions.stem: What is the main mechanism of action of pyrimethamine in treating Toxoplasma gondii?
questions.explanation: Pyrimethamine inhibits dihydrofolate reductase → blocks folate synthesis (with sulfadiazine + folinic acid).

questions.stem: Where does the primary localization of Entamoeba histolytica occur in the body?
questions.explanation: E. histolytica primarily infects the colon (then can spread to the liver).

questions.stem: Which of the following protozoa produces cysts?
questions.explanation: Entamoeba forms cysts; Trichomonas has no cyst stage; Leishmania/Plasmodium use different stages.

questions.stem: The term "tachyzoite" refers to which parasite?
questions.explanation: Tachyzoite = the rapidly dividing active form of Toxoplasma gondii.

questions.stem: Which statement about Wolbachia is correct?

options.t: It is a bacterial endosymbiont of filarial worms
It is a protozoan that causes malaria
It is the infective larval stage of hookworm
It is a fungal pathogen of the skin

questions.explanation: Wolbachia is an intracellular bacterial endosymbiont of filariae (target of doxycycline).

questions.stem: What determines the ability of Onchocerca volvulus to cause blindness?

options.t: The anatomical location of the blackfly bite (proximity to the eyes)
The blood group of the host

questions.explanation: Microfilariae migrate locally; bites nearer the head/eyes increase ocular involvement → blindness.

questions.stem: What is cysticercosis caused by?

options.t: Ingestion of food contaminated with human feces (Taenia solium eggs)
The bite of the Chrysops fly
Penetration of larvae through the skin

questions.explanation: Cysticercosis results from ingesting T. solium EGGS (fecally contaminated food/autoinfection); eating pork cysticerci causes intestinal taeniasis instead.

questions.stem: Which parasite is most likely to cause a hyperinfection cycle through autoinfection?
questions.explanation: Strongyloides has an autoinfection cycle → hyperinfection/dissemination in immunocompromised hosts.

questions.stem: Which parasite causes the formation of hydatid cysts mainly in the liver?
questions.explanation: E. granulosus forms unilocular hydatid cysts, predominantly hepatic.

questions.stem: Which symptoms may occur with infection by Ancylostoma duodenale (hookworm)?
questions.explanation: Chronic intestinal blood loss → iron-deficiency anemia and malnutrition.

questions.stem: What is the infective form of Ascaris lumbricoides for humans?
questions.explanation: Humans are infected by ingesting the embryonated (fertilized) egg.

questions.stem: Which parasite can cause elephantiasis?
questions.explanation: W. bancrofti (lymphatic filariasis) causes elephantiasis.

questions.stem: Which statement about Wuchereria bancrofti is NOT correct?

options.t: Human infection occurs through penetration of larvae at the hair follicles
Adult parasites colonize the afferent lymphatic vessels of lymph nodes
The larvae do not enter the alveolar space of the lungs
Human infection occurs through an arthropod (mosquito) vector

questions.explanation: W. bancrofti is transmitted by a MOSQUITO bite — not via hair follicles; that statement is incorrect.

questions.stem: How is the nematode Trichinella spiralis transmitted?

options.t: Skin penetration by larvae from soil

questions.explanation: Trichinella is acquired by eating raw/undercooked meat containing encysted larvae.

questions.stem: Which statements about Trichinella are correct?

options.t: It can cause severe damage to the central nervous system
There is no effective antiparasitic drug for the encysted larvae
It is transmitted via the fecal-oral route

questions.explanation: Trichinella larvae can reach the CNS/heart and there is no effective drug for encysted larvae; it is meat-borne (not fecal-oral) and does not cause blindness.

questions.stem: Which statement regarding Schistosoma haematobium is FALSE?

options.t: The parasite’s eggs are released into the environment with the feces
Transmission occurs through direct skin penetration by cercariae
Chronic schistosomiasis is caused by the inflammatory reaction to eggs retained in tissues

questions.explanation: S. haematobium eggs are shed in URINE (→ bladder pathology), not feces — so that statement is false.

questions.stem: How is Fasciola hepatica transmitted to humans?
questions.explanation: Humans ingest metacercariae encysted on aquatic plants (e.g. watercress).

questions.stem: Which statement about Enterobius vermicularis is NOT correct?

options.t: Diagnosis is best made by examining a stool ova-and-parasite sample
The hallmark symptom is perianal nocturnal itching
Transmission is fecal-oral with frequent autoinfection
Eggs are typically flattened on one side

questions.explanation: Pinworm is diagnosed by the perianal scotch-TAPE test (eggs are rarely found in stool) — so the stool-sample statement is incorrect.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the causative agent of American trypanosomiasis (Chagas disease)?
questions.explanation: T. cruzi (reduviid "kissing" bug) causes Chagas disease.
